title: How to Use Intuition When Making Relationship Decisions
meta_description: Trust your gut in love. This article shows how your sensitivity is a relationship superpower, not a flaw.
og_title: How to Use Intuition When Making Relationship Decisions
og_description: Trust your gut in love. This article shows how your sensitivity is a relationship superpower, not a flaw.
body:
You've Been Told You're "Too Sensitive" - But That's Your Superpower in Love

Let me guess. You've been in relationships where you felt something was off, but you couldn't prove it. You had that gut feeling - that sinking, twisting sensation in your stomach - but you ignored it because everyone told you that you were "overthinking" or "too emotional." And then, weeks or months later, you found out you were right all along. Sound familiar?

You're not broken. You're not too sensitive. Your nervous system is just calibrated for a wider range of input than most people's. And when it comes to relationship decisions, that's not a weakness - it's your most underused asset.

I'm Kalesh, and I've spent years helping people like you learn to trust what they already know. The problem isn't that you don't have intuition. The problem is that you've been trained to doubt it. Every time someone said "you're being dramatic" or "stop reading into things," they were teaching you to silence the very voice that could save you from heartbreak. Let's fix that.

Right?! The world has been gaslighting you out of your own knowing. But here's the truth: your intuition is a survival mechanism. It's not woo-woo magic - it's your brain processing information faster than your conscious mind can keep up. And in relationships, where stakes are high and emotions run deep, that split-second knowing can be the difference between a connection that nourishes you and one that drains you dry.

"Your intuition isn't trying to punish you - it's trying to protect you. The ache in your chest isn't anxiety. It's a message. And the only way to stop the ache is to stop ignoring the message."

Why Your Intuition Goes Silent in Relationships

Here's the thing most people don't tell you. Your intuition doesn't disappear when you fall in love. It gets louder. But so does your fear. And when you're attached to someone, your brain starts playing tricks on you.

You've probably experienced this: you're in a relationship, and everything seems fine on paper. They're kind, they're attentive, they say all the right things. But something feels... off. You can't name it. You can't prove it. And every time you try to bring it up, you feel crazy because you don't have evidence.

That's because intuition doesn't speak in evidence. It speaks in sensations. A tightness in your chest. A sudden urge to pull away. A feeling of dread when you're about to see them. These aren't random - they're your body's way of telling you something that your mind hasn't caught up to yet.

But here's where it gets tricky. Your attachment system - that part of you that craves connection and fears abandonment - can hijack your intuition. When you're scared of being alone, your brain will start rationalizing away those gut feelings. "They're just busy." "I'm being needy." "It's my past trauma talking." Sound familiar?

The key isn't to silence your fear. It's to learn the difference between fear that's protecting you and fear that's keeping you stuck. And that takes practice.

The First Step: Stop Treating Intuition Like a Magic 8-Ball

Most people make the same mistake. They treat intuition like a fortune-telling device. They want to know: "Is this person the one?" "Will we stay together?" "Should I leave?" And then they get frustrated when the answer isn't clear.

But intuition doesn't work that way. It's not about predicting the future. It's about reading the present. Your intuition can tell you how you feel right now, in this moment, with this person. It can tell you if something is aligned or misaligned. But it can't tell you what's going to happen in six months because that future doesn't exist yet.

So the first thing you need to do is stop asking your intuition big, vague questions. Start asking specific, grounded ones. Instead of "Should I marry this person?" try "How does my body feel when I think about spending the rest of my life with them?" Instead of "Is this relationship right?" try "Do I feel safe being myself around them?"

Does that land? Good. Because this shift in questioning is everything.

One of the best tools I've found for this kind of work is You Are a Badass at Making Money by Jen Sincero. It's not specifically about relationships, but it's a masterclass in trusting yourself enough to make big decisions. See on Amazon (paid link)

How to Actually Hear Your Intuition (When Your Mind Won't Shut Up)

I'm going to be real with you. Your mind is going to fight this. It's going to throw up every logical argument it can think of. "But they have a good job." "But we've been together for three years." "But what if I never find anyone else?" That's your survival brain trying to keep you safe in the only way it knows how - by keeping you in the familiar, even if the familiar is painful.

So how do you hear your intuition through all that noise? You learn to separate the signal from the static. Here's how:

1. Get Out of Your Head and Into Your Body

Intuition lives in the body, not the mind. If you're trying to figure out a relationship decision by thinking about it, you're going to get stuck in an endless loop of pros and cons. Instead, bring your attention to your physical sensations.

Close your eyes. Think about the decision you're facing. Now scan your body from head to toe. Where do you feel tension? Where do you feel expansion? Your body is always telling you the truth, even when your mind is lying.

A tight chest usually means fear or anxiety. A relaxed belly means safety. A sinking feeling in your gut means something is off. A lightness in your shoulders means relief. Learn to read these signals like a map. They'll never steer you wrong.

2. Use the "Future Self" Trick

This one is powerful. Imagine yourself six months from now, having made the decision you're considering. How does that version of you feel? Are they relieved? Regretful? Peaceful? Angry?

Your future self has access to information your current self doesn't. They know how things turned out. So tune into them. Ask them: "Did I make the right choice?" And then listen for the answer - not in words, but in feelings.

If the thought of staying with this person makes your future self feel heavy and trapped, that's your answer. If the thought of leaving makes them feel light and free, that's your answer too. Trust it.

3. Practice on Small Decisions First

You wouldn't run a marathon without training first. So don't expect to make life-altering relationship decisions based on intuition if you haven't practiced on smaller things.

Start with low-stakes choices. What do you want for dinner? Should you go to that party? Should you call that friend back? Check in with your body for each option. Notice which one feels more aligned. The more you practice, the stronger your intuition becomes.

For a deeper dive into this kind of body-based decision making, I recommend The Body Keeps the Score by Bessel van der Kolk. It's not a light read, but it will change how you understand the connection between your body and your emotions. See on Amazon (paid link)

The Hardest Part: When Your Intuition Says Leave, But Your Heart Says Stay

This is where most people get stuck. You know the relationship isn't working. Your body is screaming at you to leave. But your heart is still attached. You love them. You have history. You've invested years. How do you walk away from that?

Here's the truth I need you to hear: Love is not enough. You can love someone deeply and still know that being with them is destroying you. Intuition doesn't ask you to stop loving them. It asks you to love yourself more.

When you're in this place, the decision feels impossible because both options hurt. Staying hurts because you're not fulfilled. Leaving hurts because you're losing someone you love. So you freeze. You stay in the limbo, hoping something will change.

But nothing changes until you do. And the only way out is through.

One thing that helps is to stop framing it as "leaving them" and start framing it as "choosing yourself." Because that's what it really is. Your intuition isn't trying to punish them. It's trying to save you. And you deserve to be saved.

Another perspective shift that's helped many of my students comes from Attached by Amir Levine and Rachel Heller. It breaks down how different attachment styles affect your decision-making in relationships. See on Amazon (paid link)

How to Know If It's Intuition or Fear

This is the million-dollar question, isn't it? How do you know if that feeling in your gut is a genuine warning or just your anxiety acting up?

Here's a simple test: Fear feels tight, contracted, and urgent. It's the voice that says "You have to decide NOW or you'll lose everything." Intuition feels calm, grounded, and patient. It's the voice that says "I already know what I need to know. I'm just waiting for my mind to catch up."

Fear is reactive. Intuition is responsive. Fear pushes you to act from a place of lack. Intuion guides you from a place of knowing.

Another way to tell: Fear is usually loud and repetitive. It keeps replaying the same scenarios over and over in your head. "What if I'm wrong? What if I regret this? What if I never find anyone else?" Intuition is quieter. It doesn't need to repeat itself because it knows it's right. It's a single, clear "no" or "yes" that doesn't change no matter how much you question it.

If you're still unsure, try this: Imagine you've made the decision. Sit with it for 24 hours. Don't act on it. Just let it sit. Notice how your body responds over time. Does the feeling get stronger or weaker? Does it feel more like relief or more like dread?

Your intuition will become clearer the longer you sit with it. Fear will start to fade as you realize the world didn't end. Trust the one that stays.

What to Do When Your Intuition Conflicts With Someone Else's Needs

This is a big one, especially for people who are used to putting others first. You've been the caretaker, the peacemaker, the one who sacrifices their own needs to keep everyone else happy. And now your intuition is telling you something that's going to disappoint someone you care about.

It feels selfish, doesn't it? Like you're doing something wrong just by listening to yourself.

But here's the thing: You are not responsible for other people's feelings. You are responsible for your own choices. And when you make a choice based on your intuition, you're not hurting anyone. You're just honoring your own truth. If someone else gets upset because you're taking care of yourself, that's their issue to deal with, not yours.

I know that's easier said than done. But I want you to try something. The next time you feel that pull to ignore your intuition because it might upset someone, ask yourself: "If I were my own best friend, what would I tell me to do?"

You'd tell your best friend to protect their peace. You'd tell them to trust their gut. You'd tell them that they deserve to be in a relationship that feels good, not one that feels like a constant negotiation of their own needs.

So why are you treating yourself any differently?

For more on setting boundaries and trusting yourself in relationships, check out Set Boundaries, Find Peace by Nedra Glover Tawwab. It's a practical guide that will help you stop second-guessing yourself. See on Amazon (paid link)

A Practical Process for Using Intuition in Relationship Decisions

Let me give you a step-by-step process you can use right now, whether you're deciding to start a relationship, stay in one, or leave one.

Step 1: Create Space

You can't hear your intuition when you're distracted. Turn off your phone. Go for a walk. Sit in silence. Give yourself at least 15 minutes of uninterrupted time to just be with yourself. No music, no podcasts, no distractions.

Step 2: Ask the Right Question

Instead of "What should I do?" ask "What do I already know?" This shifts you out of problem-solving mode and into listening mode. Your intuition already knows the answer. You just need to hear it.

Step 3: Feel Into Each Option

Imagine each possible decision as a separate path. Walk down each one in your mind. Notice how your body feels on each path. Does one feel lighter? Heavier? More expansive? More constricted? The one that feels most aligned is the one your intuition is pointing toward.

Step 4: Test It

Once you think you've identified the right decision, test it. Say it out loud. "I'm going to stay in this relationship." "I'm going to end this relationship." "I'm going to tell them how I feel." Notice the physical reaction in your body. If it feels like a release, like a sigh of relief, you're on the right track. If it feels like a knot tightening, you're not.

Step 5: Act

This is the hardest step. Once you know, you have to act. Not next week. Not when you're ready. Now. Intuition loses its power the longer you wait. The longer you sit on what you know, the more your mind will talk you out of it. So act while the knowing is fresh.

What to Do When You Ignore Your Intuition (Because You Will)

Let's be real. You're going to ignore your intuition sometimes. We all do. You're going to stay in a relationship longer than you should. You're going to date someone your gut tells you is wrong. You're going to make decisions based on fear instead of trust. And when that happens, you're going to beat yourself up for it.

Don't.

Your intuition doesn't punish you for ignoring it. It just gets louder. Every time you ignore it, it comes back with more force, more clarity, more urgency. It's not trying to make you feel bad. It's trying to get your attention. So instead of drowning in shame, use it as a learning experience.

Ask yourself: "What was I afraid of that made me ignore my gut?" "What would I do differently next time?" "What did I learn about myself from this experience?"

That's how you build trust with yourself. Not by being perfect, but by showing up for yourself even when you mess up.

The Bottom Line

Your intuition is not the enemy. It's not something to fear or control. It's a part of you that has been trying to protect you your whole life. And when it comes to relationship decisions, it's the most reliable guide you have.

The people who trust their intuition aren't special. They're not more gifted or more spiritual than you. They've just practiced. They've learned to listen to the quiet voice inside instead of the loud voice of fear. And you can learn to do the same.

So the next time you're faced with a relationship decision and you feel that pull in your gut, don't push it away. Don't rationalize it. Don't ask everyone else for their opinion. Sit with it. Listen to it. Trust it. Because deep down, you already know what to do. You always have.

And if you need more help learning to trust yourself, I've put together a free assessment that will show you exactly where you're getting stuck in your decision-making. Take the assessment here.
